subject: Physics
q: A plane is in level flight at constant speed and each of its two wings has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of the air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the lower wing surface and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>252</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the upper wing surface, the mass of the plane is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Take air density to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A plane is in level flight at constant speed and each of its two wings has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of the air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the lower wing surface and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>252</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> over the upper wing surface, the mass of the plane is ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Take air density to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Total wing span area, A=2×40=80 m2

Using Bernoulli's equation, we get uplift force as

AP2-P1=12ρV12-V22A

⇒mg=12×1702-502×80

⇒mg=2400×40

⇒m=9600 kg

subject: Physics
q: A small ball of mass M and density ρ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density ρ0. After some time, the ball falls with a constant velocity. What is the viscous force on the ball?
question: A small ball of mass M and density ρ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density ρ0. After some time, the ball falls with a constant velocity. What is the viscous force on the ball?
solution:
The buoyant force is given by 

B=Vρ0g

where V is the volume of the ball. The weight of the ball

W=Mg=Vρg, is acting in the opposite direction of the buoyant force . Hence, at terminal velocity

Mg-B=Fv

⇒B+Fv=Mg

⇒Fv=Vρg-Vρ0g⇒Fv=ρVg(1-ρ0ρ)⇒Fv=Mg(1-ρ0ρ)

solution: F1F2=14

subject: Physics
q: Two liquids A and B have $\theta_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\theta_{\mathrm{B}}$ as contact angles in a capillary tube. If $K=\cos \theta_A / \cos \theta_B$. then identify the correct statement:
question: Two liquids A and B have $\theta_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\theta_{\mathrm{B}}$ as contact angles in a capillary tube. If $K=\cos \theta_A / \cos \theta_B$. then identify the correct statement:
options: $K$ is negative, then liquid $A$ and liquid $B$ have convex meniscus.
$K$ is negative, then liquid $A$ and liquid $B$ have concave meniscus.
K is negative, then liquid A has concave meniscus and liquid $B$ has convex meniscus
K is zero, then liquid A has convex meniscus and liquid B has concave meniscus.
solution:
$\mathrm{k}=\frac{\cos \theta_{\mathrm{A}}}{\cos \theta_{\mathrm{B}}}$
It is negative when $\cos \theta_A \& \cos \theta_B$ are of opposite sign. so option (3)

subject: Physics
q: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
question: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
solution:
The density of any point inside the sphere is

ρ=ρ01-r2R2   0<r≤R

weight = buoyant force
mg = B

mg=ρLVg
∫ρ4πr2drg=ρL43πR3g
∫0Rρ01-r2R24πr2dr=ρL43πR3
∫0Rρ04πr2-r4R2dr=ρ04πr33-r55R20R=ρL43πR3
25ρ0=ρL

subject: Physics
q:
Water from a tap emerges vertically downwards with an initial speed of  1.0 ms-1 . The cross - sectional area of the tap is  10-4 m2 . Assume that the pressure is constant throughout the stream of water and that the flow is streamlined. The cross - sectional area of the stream, 0.15 m below the tap would be:
(Take  g=10 ms-2 )
question:
Water from a tap emerges vertically downwards with an initial speed of  1.0 ms-1 . The cross - sectional area of the tap is  10-4 m2 . Assume that the pressure is constant throughout the stream of water and that the flow is streamlined. The cross - sectional area of the stream, 0.15 m below the tap would be:
(Take  g=10 ms-2 )
solution:
Velocity of water after falling through distance 0.15 m is
v2=u2+2as
=1+2×10×0.15
v2=4
v=2 m/s
From equation of continuity
A1v1=A2v2
A2=10-4×12=5×10-5 m2

subject: Physics
q: A glass tumbler having inner depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is kept on a table. A student starts pouring water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> into it while looking at the surface of water from the above. When he feels that the tumbler is half filled, he stops pouring water. Up to what height, the tumbler is actually filled ?
question: A glass tumbler having inner depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is kept on a table. A student starts pouring water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> into it while looking at the surface of water from the above. When he feels that the tumbler is half filled, he stops pouring water. Up to what height, the tumbler is actually filled ?
solution:
Height of water observed by observer
=Hμw=H(4/3)=3H4

Height of air observed by observer =17.5-H

According to question, both height observed by observer is same.
3H4=17.5-H

⇒  H=10 cm

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small droplets of water. The surface energy will become :
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small droplets of water. The surface energy will become :
solution:
Lets say radius of small droplets is r and that of big drop is R. Total volume of the drops will remains unchanged. Therefore, 

43πR3=100043πr3

⇒R=10r

Now, initial surface energy is, Ui=10004πr2 S and the final surface energy is, 

Uf=4πR2 S

=1004πr2S

Clearly, Uf=110Ui.

subject: Physics
q: If it takes 5 minutes to fill a 15 litre bucket from a water tap of diameter <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then the Reynolds number for the flow is (density of water = <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>  </mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi></mrow></mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>y</mi><mi> </mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mi> </mi><mi>w</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>s</mi></math> ) close to:
question: If it takes 5 minutes to fill a 15 litre bucket from a water tap of diameter <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then the Reynolds number for the flow is (density of water = <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>  </mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi></mrow></mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>y</mi><mi> </mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi><mi> </mi><mi>w</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>s</mi></math> ) close to:
solution:
Reynolds number

R=ρvDη

D =  Diameter of litre

Also rate of flow = volumetime=A v

Vt= π D24×v ⇒v=4VπD2t

∴    R= ρ Dη×4 Vπ D2 t

=4 ρ Vπ η D t

=4×103×15×10-3π×10-3×2×5×60 π×102

=10000π ≈5500

subject: Physics
q: In a cylindrical water tank, there are two small holes $A$ and $B$ on the wall at a depth of $h_1$, from the surface of water and at a height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at heigh $H$ from the bottom of water tank. Water coming out from both holes strikes the ground at the same point $S$. Find the ratio of $h_1$ and $h_2$
question: In a cylindrical water tank, there are two small holes $A$ and $B$ on the wall at a depth of $h_1$, from the surface of water and at a height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at height of $h_2$ from the bottom of water tank. Surface of water is at heigh $H$ from the bottom of water tank. Water coming out from both holes strikes the ground at the same point $S$. Find the ratio of $h_1$ and $h_2$
solution:
Range is same for both holes
$$
\therefore 2 \sqrt{\left(\mathrm{H}-h_1\right) h_1}=2 \sqrt{\left(\mathrm{H}-h_2\right) h_2}
$$
Squaring both sides,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 4\left(H-h_1\right) \mathrm{h}_1=4\left(H-h_2\right) h_2 \\
& H h_1-h_1^2=H h_2-h_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving we get,
$$
H=h_1+h_2
$$
Hence, the ratio of $\frac{h_1}{h_2}$ depends on $H$.

subject: Physics
q: A capillary tube is immersed vertically in water and the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When this arrangement is taken into a mine of depth d, the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. If R is the radius of earth, the ratio <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> is :
question: A capillary tube is immersed vertically in water and the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When this arrangement is taken into a mine of depth d, the height of the water column is <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. If R is the radius of earth, the ratio <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> is :
solution:
We know that

h = 2 T cos θ ρ gr

∴    x = 2 T cos θ ρ gr

Now, value of geff  at depth 'd' is

g eff = g 1 - d R

∴    y = 2 T cos θ ρ g eff · r

∴    x y = g eff g = 1 - d R

subject: Physics
q: A small soap bubble of radius 4cm is trapped inside another bubble of radius 6cm without any contact. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
question: A small soap bubble of radius 4cm is trapped inside another bubble of radius 6cm without any contact. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
solution:
P2−P1=4Tr1      ....(1)P1−P0=4Tr2  .....(2)adding (1) & (2)P2−P0=4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4T6+4T4

for another bubble of radius r

P2= P0+4Tr

⇒   1r=16+14

⇒   r=2.4 cm

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the density and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> is coefficient of viscosity of fluid which flows with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> in the pipe of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, the correct formula for Reynolds number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the density and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> is coefficient of viscosity of fluid which flows with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> in the pipe of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, the correct formula for Reynolds number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is
solution:
Reynolds number is given by, Re=ρvdη, where, v=flow velocity, d=hydraulic diameter of the pipe, ρ=density of the fluid & η=coefficient of viscosity of the fluid.

Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces within a fluid which is subjected to relative internal movement due to different fluid velocities.

subject: Physics
q: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
question: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
solution:
As the volume of the mercury will remain same, therefore

43πR3=12543πr3⇒R=5r

Now, increase in surface energy

∆U=125×S×4πr2-S×4πR2

 =0.45×4π×10-3212525-1

=4×0.45×4π×10-6

=2.26×10-5J

subject: Physics
q: A liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> flows smoothly through a horizontal pipe that tapers in cross-sectional area from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of the pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. The rate of flow of liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> flows smoothly through a horizontal pipe that tapers in cross-sectional area from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The pressure difference between the wide and narrow sections of the pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></math>. The rate of flow of liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
From the equation of continuity, v2=2v1.

Applying the Bernoulli's Theorem,

P1+ρv122=P2+ρ4v122  ⇒P1-P2=3ρv122

⇒4500=3×750×v122  ⇒v1=2 m s-1

Now volume flow rate,Q=Av1=1.2×10-2×2=24×10-3 m3 s-1

subject: Physics
q: Suppose you have taken a dilute solution of oleic acid in such a way that its concentration becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of oleic acid per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of the solution. Then you make a thin film of this solution (monomolecular thickness) of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by considering <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> spherical drops of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mn>40</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the thickness of oleic acid layer will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________ .
question: Suppose you have taken a dilute solution of oleic acid in such a way that its concentration becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of oleic acid per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of the solution. Then you make a thin film of this solution (monomolecular thickness) of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by considering <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> spherical drops of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mrow><mn>40</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the thickness of oleic acid layer will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________ .
solution:
4tT=100×43πr3

=100×4π3×340π×10-9=10-8 cm3

tT=25×10-10 cm=25×10-12 m

t0=0.01tT=25×10-14 m⇒x=25.

subject: Physics
q: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through a viscous medium of negligible density has terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Another ball of the same mass but of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through the same viscous medium will have terminal velocity:
question: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through a viscous medium of negligible density has terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Another ball of the same mass but of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through the same viscous medium will have terminal velocity:
solution:
Ignoring buoyancy force, we can write terminal velocity as 

mg=6πηrv

⇒v∝1r (as mass is constant)

Now, vv'=r'r

As r'=2r

So v'=v2

subject: Physics
q: Two soap bubbles of radius 2 cm and 4 cm , respectively, are in contact with each other. The radius of curvature of the common surface, in cm , is ______ .
question: Two soap bubbles of radius 2 cm and 4 cm , respectively, are in contact with each other. The radius of curvature of the common surface, in cm , is ______ .
solution: $R=\frac{R_2 R_1}{R_2-R_1}=\frac{4 \times 2}{2}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: What will be the nature of flow of water from a circular tap, when its flow rate increased from 0.18 L min-1 to 0.48 L min-1? The radius of the tap and viscosity of water are 0.5 cm and 10-3 Pa s, respectively. (Density of water : 103 kg m-3)
question: What will be the nature of flow of water from a circular tap, when its flow rate increased from 0.18 L min-1 to 0.48 L min-1? The radius of the tap and viscosity of water are 0.5 cm and 10-3 Pa s, respectively. (Density of water : 103 kg m-3)
solution:
The nature of flow is determined by Reynolds
Number. 

Re=ρvDη

ρ→ density of fluid ;  η→ coefficient of velocityv→ velocity of flow D→ Diameterofpipe 

From NCERT 

If Re<1000 → flow is steady

1000<Rp<2000→ flow becomes unsteady

Re>2000→ flow is turbulent 

Reinitial =103×0.18×10-3π×0.5×10-22×60×1×10-210-3

=382.16

Re final=103×0.48×10-3π×0.5×10-22×60×1×10-210-3

=1019.09

subject: Physics
q: Glycerin of density 1.25×103 kg m–3 is flowing through the conical section of pipe. The area of cross-section of the pipe at its ends are 10 cm2 and 5 cm2 and pressure drop across its length is 3 N m–2. The rate of flow of glycerine through the pipe is x×10–5 m3 s–1. The value of x is _____.
question: Glycerin of density 1.25×103 kg m–3 is flowing through the conical section of pipe. The area of cross-section of the pipe at its ends are 10 cm2 and 5 cm2 and pressure drop across its length is 3 N m–2. The rate of flow of glycerine through the pipe is x×10–5 m3 s–1. The value of x is _____.
solution:
It is given that

A1=10 cm2; A2=5 cm2

∆P=3 N m-2

By continuity equation,

A1v1=A2v2⇒v1=A2v2A1   ...(i)

By Bernoulli's equation,

12ρv22-12ρv12=∆P   ...(ii)

Using equation (i) in (ii)

∆P=ρ2(v22-A2v2A12)⇒∆P=ρv2221-A2A12⇒3=1.25×103×v2221-14⇒v22=6×41.25×103×3⇒v2=8×10-2 m s-1

So, the discharge rate is,

A2v2=x×10-5⇒x=5×10-4×8×10-210-5=4

subject: Physics
q: A water spray gun is attached to a hose of cross sectional area $30$ cm$^2$. The gun comprises of $10$ perforations each of cross sectional area $15$ mm$^2$. If the water flows in the hose with the speed of $50$ cm/s, calculate the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation. (Neglect any edge effects)
question: A water spray gun is attached to a hose of cross sectional area $30$ cm$^2$. The gun comprises of $10$ perforations each of cross sectional area $15$ mm$^2$. If the water flows in the hose with the speed of $50$ cm/s, calculate the speed at which the water flows out from each perforation. (Neglect any edge effects)
solution:
From the equation of continuity:

$A_1 v_1 = n A_2 v_2$

Here,
$A_1 = 30 \text{ cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$v_1 = 50 \text{ cm/s} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
$n = 10$
$A_2 = 15 \text{ mm}^2 = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

Substituting the given values:

$(30 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.5 = 10 \times (15 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_2$

$15 \times 10^{-4} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times v_2$

$15 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times v_2$

$v_2 = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ m/s}$

Answer: $10$ m/s

subject: Physics
q: Water from a pipe is coming at a rate of <math><mn>100</mn></math> liters per minute. If the radius of the pipe is <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Reynolds number for the flow is of the order of: (density of water = <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> coefficient of viscosity of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Water from a pipe is coming at a rate of <math><mn>100</mn></math> liters per minute. If the radius of the pipe is <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Reynolds number for the flow is of the order of: (density of water = <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> coefficient of viscosity of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Volume flow rate =πr2v=100 lmin
⇒πr2v=1600 m3/sec
⇒v=1600 πr2 m/sec
Reynolds number Re=ρvDη
=10001×10-3×1600 πr2×2r
=20000.6π5×10-2=21220.66=2.1×104

q: A soap bubble, blown by a mechanical pump at the mouth of a tube increases in volume with time at a constant rate. The graph that correctly depicts the time dependence of pressure inside the bubble is given by:
question: A soap bubble, blown by a mechanical pump at the mouth of a tube increases in volume with time at a constant rate. The graph that correctly depicts the time dependence of pressure inside the bubble is given by:
solution:
dVdt=k

⇒V=kt

⇒43πr3=kt

⇒r=3k4π13t13

P-P0=4Tr

⇒P-P0=4T.3k4π13.1t13 ⇒P=4T.3k4π13.1t13 +P0

subject: Physics
q:
Pressure inside a soap bubble is greater than the pressure outside by an amount :
(given : $\mathrm{R}=$ Radius of bubble $\mathrm{S}=$ Surface tension of bubble)
question:
Pressure inside a soap bubble is greater than the pressure outside by an amount :
(given : $\mathrm{R}=$ Radius of bubble $\mathrm{S}=$ Surface tension of bubble)
solution:
There are two liquid-air surfaces in bubble so
$\Delta \mathrm{P}=2\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=\frac{4 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}}$

subject: Physics
q: Work done in increasing the size of a soap bubble from a radius of $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ to $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is nearly (Surface tension of soap solution $=0.03 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ ):
question: Work done in increasing the size of a soap bubble from a radius of $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ to $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is nearly (Surface tension of soap solution $=0.03 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ ):
solution: $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{T} \times \Delta \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{T} \times 8 \pi\left(\mathrm{r}_2{ }^2-\mathrm{r}_1{ }^2\right)=0.4 \pi \mathrm{mJ}$

subject: Physics
q: A sphere of relative density $\sigma$ and diameter $D$ has concentric cavity of diameter $d$. The ratio of $\frac{D}{d}$, if it just floats on water in a tank is :
question: A sphere of relative density $\sigma$ and diameter $D$ has concentric cavity of diameter $d$. The ratio of $\frac{D}{d}$, if it just floats on water in a tank is :
solution:
weight $(w)=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3-\mathrm{d}^3}{8}\right) \sigma \mathrm{g}$
Buoyant force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)=1 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3}{8}\right) \cdot \mathrm{g}$
For Just Float $\Rightarrow \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(D^3-d^3\right) \sigma=D^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{d^3}{D^3}=\frac{1}{\sigma} \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{1}{\sigma}=\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-1}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
question: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
solution:
$$ \text { Let the rate of falling water level be }-\frac{d h}{d t} $$
Initially at $t=0 ; h=h$ $$ t=t ; h=0 $$ Then, $A\left(-\frac{d h}{d t}\right)=\pi a^2 \cdot v$ $$ d t=-\frac{A}{\pi a^2 \sqrt{2 g h}} d h $$ $[\because$ velocity of efflux of liquid $v=\sqrt{2 g h}]$ Integrating both sides $$ \int_0^t d t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \int_h^0 h^{-1 / 2} d h $$ $$ [t]_0^t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \cdot\left[\frac{h^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right]_h^0 $$ $$ t=\frac{\sqrt{2} A}{\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{g}} $$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a cylindrical tank of radius 1 m is filled with water. The top surface of water is at 15 m from the bottom of the cylinder. There is a hole on the wall of cylinder at a height of 5 m from the bottom. A force of 5×105 N is applied an the top surface of water using a piston. The speed of efflux from the hole will be :
(given atmospheric pressure PA=1.01×105 Pa, density of water ρw=1000 kg m-3 and gravitational acceleration g=10 m s-2)
question:
Consider a cylindrical tank of radius 1 m is filled with water. The top surface of water is at 15 m from the bottom of the cylinder. There is a hole on the wall of cylinder at a height of 5 m from the bottom. A force of 5×105 N is applied an the top surface of water using a piston. The speed of efflux from the hole will be :
(given atmospheric pressure PA=1.01×105 Pa, density of water ρw=1000 kg m-3 and gravitational acceleration g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Assuming there is no atmospheric pressure on piston, P1=FπR2 and P2=P0.

Applying Bernoulli's equation at point 1 and 2,

p1+ρgh1+12ρv12=p2+ρgh2+12ρv22

⇒FπR2+ρgh1-h2=P0+12ρv22

⇒v2=2FπR2ρ-2P0ρ+2gh1-h2

v2=2×5×1053.14×12×1000-2×1.01×1051000+2×10×10

≈17.8 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A wooden block floating in a bucket of water has <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of its volume submerged. When certain amount of an oil is poured into the bucket, it is found that the block is just under the oil surface with half of its volume under water and half in oil. The density of oil relative to that of water is:
question: A wooden block floating in a bucket of water has <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of its volume submerged. When certain amount of an oil is poured into the bucket, it is found that the block is just under the oil surface with half of its volume under water and half in oil. The density of oil relative to that of water is:
solution:
Case-1, σ Vg=ρw45Vg …….(I)
Case-2, σ Vg=ρwV2g+ρoV2g …….(II)
From eq (I) & (II),
45ρw=ρw2+ρo2
45-12ρw=ρo2
8-510ρw=ρo2
ρoρw=35=0.6

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
Statement II : The rise of a liquid in a capillary tube does not depend on the inner radius of the tube.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
Statement II : The rise of a liquid in a capillary tube does not depend on the inner radius of the tube.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
solution:
Statement I is correct as we know contact angle depends on cohesine and adhesive forces.
Statement II is incorrect because height of liquid is given by $h==\frac{2 T \cos \theta_C}{\rho g r}$ where $r$ is radius of Tube (assuming length of capillary is sufficient).
